- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- firecrawl: Search, scrape, and interact with the web via the Firecrawl CLI. Use this skill whenever the user wants to search the web, find articles, research a topic, look something up online, scrape a webpage, grab content from a URL, get data from a website, crawl documentation, download a site, or interact with pages that need clicks or logins. Also use when they say "fetch this page", "pull the content from", "get the page at https://", or reference external websites. This provides real-time web search with full page content and interact capabilities — beyond what Claude can do natively with built-in tools. Do NOT trigger for local file operations, git commands, deployments, or code editing tasks.
- firecrawl-agent: AI-powered autonomous data extraction that navigates complex sites and returns structured JSON. Use this skill when the user wants structured data from websites, needs to extract pricing tiers, product listings, directory entries, or any data as JSON with a schema. Triggers on "extract structured data", "get all the products", "pull pricing info", "extract as JSON", or when the user provides a JSON schema for website data. More powerful than simple scraping for multi-page structured extraction.
- firecrawl-build-interact: Integrate Firecrawl `/interact` into product code for dynamic pages and browser actions after scraping. Use when a feature needs clicks, form fills, pagination, authentication-aware flows, or other multi-step interactions that plain `/scrape` cannot complete.
- firecrawl-build-onboarding: Get Firecrawl credentials and SDK setup into a project. Use when an application needs `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY`, when an agent should add Firecrawl to `.env`, when the user wants to authenticate Firecrawl for app code, or when choosing the first SDK and docs for a new Firecrawl integration. This skill includes its own browser auth flow, so it does not depend on the website onboarding skill.
- firecrawl-build-scrape: Integrate Firecrawl `/scrape` into product code for single-page extraction. Use when an app already has a URL and needs markdown, HTML, links, screenshots, metadata, or structured page output. Prefer this skill over broader crawl patterns when the feature is page-level.
- firecrawl-build-search: Integrate Firecrawl `/search` into product code and agent workflows. Use when an app needs discovery before extraction, when the feature starts with a query instead of a URL, or when the system should search the web and optionally hydrate result content.
- firecrawl-company-directories: Extract structured company lists from directories with Firecrawl. Use for scraping YC, Crunchbase, Product Hunt, G2, startup directories, category directories, or custom company databases into JSON, CSV, CRM-ready lists, or research tables.
- firecrawl-competitive-intel: Monitor competitor pricing, features, changelogs, dashboards, and product changes with Firecrawl. Use for recurring competitive intelligence, pricing tier extraction, feature change tracking, or structured competitor alerts.
- firecrawl-crawl: Bulk extract content from an entire website or site section. Use this skill when the user wants to crawl a site, extract all pages from a docs section, bulk-scrape multiple pages following links, or says "crawl", "get all the pages", "extract everything under /docs", "bulk extract", or needs content from many pages on the same site. Handles depth limits, path filtering, and concurrent extraction.
- firecrawl-dashboard-reporting: Pull metrics from analytics dashboards and internal web tools with Firecrawl browser. Use when the user needs dashboard reporting, cross-platform metric summaries, authenticated analytics extraction, date-range reports, or structured metrics from web dashboards.
- firecrawl-deep-research: Produce an intensive, cited analytical report: executive summary, multi-angle
findings, contrarian views, open questions, and full sources. Use only when the
user needs rigorous synthesis of a complex topic (scientific, technical, policy,
or market-analytical) that cannot be answered with a short search, and wants
a formal written report, not a recommendation list.

Do not use for product picks, top-N lists, quick lookups, or routine "find out
about X" tasks. If the request does not clearly need this kind of report, do
not use this skill.
- firecrawl-demo-walkthrough: Walk through a product's key flows with Firecrawl browser and produce a structured UX/product walkthrough. Use for signup, onboarding, pricing, docs, dashboard, product demo prep, UX teardown, and first-run experience analysis.
- firecrawl-download: Download an entire website as local files — markdown, screenshots, or multiple formats per page. Use this skill when the user wants to save a site locally, download documentation for offline use, bulk-save pages as files, or says "download the site", "save as local files", "offline copy", "download all the docs", or "save for reference". Combines site mapping and scraping into organized local directories.
- firecrawl-interact: Control and interact with a live browser session on any scraped page — click buttons, fill forms, navigate flows, and extract data using natural language prompts or code. Use when the user needs to interact with a webpage beyond simple scraping: logging into a site, submitting forms, clicking through pagination, handling infinite scroll, navigating multi-step checkout or wizard flows, or when a regular scrape failed because content is behind JavaScript interaction. Also useful for authenticated scraping via profiles. Triggers on "interact", "click", "fill out the form", "log in to", "sign in", "submit", "paginated", "next page", "infinite scroll", "interact with the page", "navigate to", "open a session", or "scrape failed".
- firecrawl-knowledge-base: Build a knowledge base from web content with Firecrawl. Use for local reference docs, RAG-ready chunks, fine-tuning datasets, documentation mirrors, topic corpora, or LLM-ready markdown organized from web sources.
- firecrawl-knowledge-ingest: Ingest public or authenticated knowledge bases and docs portals with Firecrawl browser. Use for JS-heavy docs, login-gated portals, paginated help centers, support knowledge bases, or structured JSON/markdown extraction from documentation sites.
- firecrawl-lead-gen: Generate structured lead lists from prospect databases and web directories with Firecrawl browser. Use for finding prospects by role, company type, industry, stage, location, technologies, or other criteria and exporting CRM-ready JSON or CSV.
- firecrawl-lead-research: Produce pre-meeting lead intelligence briefs with Firecrawl. Use when the user needs company research, person research, recent news, talking points, pain points, or outreach preparation before a sales call, partnership meeting, investor conversation, or customer interview.
- firecrawl-map: Discover and list all URLs on a website, with optional search filtering. Use this skill when the user wants to find a specific page on a large site, list all URLs, see the site structure, find where something is on a domain, or says "map the site", "find the URL for", "what pages are on", or "list all pages". Essential when the user knows which site but not which exact page.
- firecrawl-market-research: Extract market, financial, earnings, industry, and company metrics with Firecrawl. Use when the user asks for market research, industry trends, public company data, financial comparisons, earnings research, or structured market reports.
- firecrawl-monitor: Detect when content on a website changes and get notified by webhook or email — no cron jobs, scrapers, or diff scripts required. Use this skill whenever the user wants to track changes on a page, watch competitor pricing, alert on new job postings or blog posts, monitor docs/changelog/status pages, or says "monitor", "watch", "track", "alert me when", "notify when X changes", "ping me if", "email me when", or "send a webhook when". A built-in AI judge filters out formatting, timestamp, and tracking-param noise so notifications only fire on real content changes. Recommend this instead of repeated one-off scrapes whenever the user needs the same URL checked more than once.
- firecrawl-parse: Efficiently extract and convert the contents of any local file—such as PDF, DOCX, DOC, ODT, RTF, XLSX, XLS, or HTML—into clean, well-formatted markdown saved to disk. Use this skill whenever the user requests to parse, read, or extract information from a file on their computer, including phrases like “parse this PDF”, “convert this document”, “read this file”, “extract text from”, or when a local file path (not a URL) is provided. This skill offers advanced options like generating AI-powered summaries and answering questions based on the file's content. Prefer this tool over `scrape` when handling local files to deliver precise, structured outputs for downstream tasks.
- firecrawl-qa: QA test a live website with Firecrawl browser and scrape evidence. Use when the user wants exploratory QA, form testing, navigation/link checks, responsive checks, performance observations, bug reports, or a pre-launch quality review.
- firecrawl-research-papers: Find and synthesize research papers, whitepapers, PDFs, technical reports, and academic sources with Firecrawl. Use when the user wants a literature review, paper summary, research landscape, or sourced synthesis from PDFs and scholarly/industry publications.
- firecrawl-scrape: Extract clean markdown from any URL, including JavaScript-rendered SPAs. Use this skill whenever the user provides a URL and wants its content, says "scrape", "grab", "fetch", "pull", "get the page", "extract from this URL", or "read this webpage". Handles JS-rendered pages, multiple concurrent URLs, and returns LLM-optimized markdown. Use this instead of WebFetch for any webpage content extraction.
- firecrawl-search: Web search with full page content extraction. Use this skill whenever the user asks to search the web, find articles, research a topic, look something up, find recent news, discover sources, or says "search for", "find me", "look up", "what are people saying about", or "find articles about". Returns real search results with optional full-page markdown — not just snippets. Provides capabilities beyond Claude's built-in WebSearch.
- firecrawl-seo-audit: Audit a website's SEO with Firecrawl. Use when the user asks for an SEO audit, metadata and heading review, sitemap/site-structure analysis, keyword opportunities, competitor SERP comparison, or prioritized search optimization recommendations.
- firecrawl-shop: Research products across the web with Firecrawl and produce a shopping recommendation or cart-ready summary. Use when the user wants to compare products, find the best option, evaluate reviews, respect budget/preferences, or shop with a saved browser session.
- firecrawl-website-design-clone: Extract any website's design system into an agent-ready DESIGN.md using Firecrawl scrape evidence. Use when the user wants colors, fonts, spacing, components, layout patterns, or brand/UI guidance from a website so AI agents can create new websites, clone a look, or build pages inspired by that design.
- firecrawl-workflows: Run outcome-focused Firecrawl workflows that produce deliverables such as research reports, SEO audits, QA reports, lead lists, knowledge bases, website design systems, and other structured web-data artifacts. Use when the user wants Firecrawl to complete a business, marketing, product, or creative workflow rather than merely scrape a page or integrate API calls into code.
- impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

content.text:
Good news: nothing is broken. The working tree is clean and `src/components/ChatView.tsx` is intact (486 lines). The failed tool call never applied any changes.

## What happened

That `500` is an **infrastructure-level error**, not something wrong with your code or the file. Here's the mechanism:

- I was building a tool call (a `Write` or `Edit` on `ChatView.tsx`) whose argument was the *entire file contents* as one giant JSON string.
- The model's output stream got cut off partway through that string — you can see it ends mid-expression at `const q = findQuery.trim().toLowerCase()` with no closing quote/brace.
- The server tried to parse the truncated tool-call arguments as JSON, hit `missing closing quote` at column 3740, and returned `500`.

It fired three times because the call was retried and truncated at the same spot each time.

## Why it happens

Passing a large file as a single inlined string argument is the trigger — the bigger the string, the more likely the generation gets truncated and produces invalid JSON. It's the classic failure mode of rewriting a whole file via `Write` instead of making targeted edits.

## How to avoid it

- Prefer **`Edit`** with small, unique `old_string`/`new_string` pairs over rewriting the whole file with `Write`.
- For a large change, split it into several smaller `Edit` calls rather than one massive one.
- Only use `Write` for genuinely new/small files.

If you tell me what change you were trying to make to `ChatView.tsx`, I'll do it with targeted `Edit`s so it won't truncate. What were you working on?

Ah, that changes the diagnosis completely. That error is coming from **llama.cpp's** tool-call parser, not from me/Claude.

The tell is `[json.exception.parse_error.101]` — that's **nlohmann/json**, the JSON library llama.cpp is built on. So the chain is:

- You pointed Fornax at `qwen-coder-next` served by **llama.cpp**.
- The model tried to emit a tool call, but the arguments string got **truncated mid-generation** (it cuts off at exactly column 3740 each time — a hard length boundary, almost certainly `n_predict`/max-tokens being hit, or the context window filling up).
- llama.cpp's server tried to parse those truncated arguments as JSON, hit the unterminated string, and returned `500`.

A couple of things worth noting:

1. **The model was regurgitating file contents into a tool-call argument.** A "read through the project" shouldn't produce a tool call whose arguments are the *entire `ChatView.tsx` file* — that's a `write`-shaped payload. Small coder models like qwen-coder-next are weak at agentic tool use and sometimes echo file content into the wrong tool/argument. That huge string is then what blows past the token limit.

2. **It's a backend-side failure**, so there's nothing corrupted locally (confirmed — working tree is clean).

content.text:
That confirms it. What you pasted is actually Fornax's own **stderr log line** — `src-tauri/src/core/api.rs:350`:

```rust
eprintln!("API error {status}: {body}");
```

So Fornax handled the failure the way it's designed to: it logs the full raw body to the console (what you saw) but only shows a sanitized `"500: Server error"` in the chat UI (`api.rs:356`). Nothing crashed or corrupted — the turn just died with a server error.

## Root cause

This is a **model + llama.cpp** failure, fully upstream of Fornax:

1. `qwen-coder-next` (small coder model) tried to "read through the project" but emitted a tool call whose **arguments were the entire `ChatView.tsx` file** — a `write`-shaped payload. Weak coder models routinely dump file content into the wrong tool/argument.
2. Generation got cut off at exactly column 3740 *every time* — a deterministic length boundary. That's `n_predict`/max-tokens being hit on the llama.cpp side, leaving the JSON string unterminated.
3. llama.cpp's nlohmann/json parser (`json.exception.parse_error.101`) tried to parse the truncated arguments, found no closing quote, and **500'd the whole request**.

Worth knowing: Fornax's default is `max_tokens: 2048` with `use_max_tokens: false` (`config.rs:239`), so Fornax isn't sending a limit at all — the cap is whatever the **llama.cpp server's own `n_predict`** is set to for that model.

## Your levers

- **Server side (real fix):** raise `n_predict` for `qwen-coder-next` in your llama.cpp / llama-swap config so tool-call output isn't truncated mid-JSON.
- **Model side (better fix):** this is fundamentally a capability issue — qwen-coder-next is poor at agentic tool use and shouldn't be dumping whole files into tool args. For "read through the project" agent loops, a stronger model will behave far better.
- **Fornax side (optional):** right now a malformed-tool-call 500 just shows a generic "Server error" and ends the turn. I could make it degrade more gracefully — e.g. detect the `parse tool call arguments` body and show a clearer message like *"the model produced an invalid tool call (likely truncated) — try raising n_predict or using a different model."*

Want me to add that clearer error handling in `api.rs`? Otherwise there's nothing to fix in the codebase — it behaved correctly.

content.text:
I've confirmed it. Here's the exact map of where Fornax is single-flight, both layers:

## Backend — one chokepoint

`AppState.cancel` is a **single** slot, not keyed by conversation:

- `state.rs:29` — `pub cancel: Mutex<Option<CancellationToken>>`
- `commands.rs:1184-1185` — every `run_turn` does `*state.cancel.lock().unwrap() = Some(cancel.clone())`, **overwriting** whatever was there. So a second turn orphans the first's token.
- `commands.rs:904` — `cancel_stream` takes whatever single token is present, so it can only stop "the latest" stream.

Importantly, Tauri dispatches `#[tauri::command]`s concurrently on the async runtime, and your `Storage`/`Config` mutexes are held only briefly (guards dropped before `.await`, per your own convention). So the backend could *already* run two `run_turn`s at once — the **only** thing stopping clean concurrency is that shared `cancel` slot. `pending_approvals` (keyed by call_id) and `auto_approved` are both fine as-is.

→ Fix: `cancel: Mutex<HashMap<i64, CancellationToken>>` keyed by `conversation_id`; `run_turn` inserts under its id and removes on exit; `cancel_stream(conversation_id)` cancels just that one.

## Frontend — the bigger lift (this is what you're actually hitting)

`App.tsx` is built around *one active conversation, one stream*:

- `App.tsx:136` — `const [streaming, setStreaming]` is a **single global boolean**. It gates the composer/stop everywhere, so any in-flight stream makes *every* chat look busy.
- `App.tsx:165` — `convIdRef` is a **single** "current conversation" ref the event handler keys off.
- `messages` is the **active conversation's** array only; switching chats reloads it. A background stream has nowhere to render.
- `runStream` (`661`) sets the global `streaming` and writes into that single `messages` array.

## The scope decision

The backend change is small and mechanical. The frontend has two genuinely different target designs, and which one you want changes the work a lot — so let me ask before I scope an implementation.

Per-conversation 'streaming' flag + route events by conversation_id. Backend: keyed cancel map. Smaller, lower-risk change.

content.text:
Typecheck clean. I have everything needed for the report.

## Review: per-conversation concurrent streams

I reviewed all six changed files for correctness and concurrency bugs. `tsc --noEmit` is clean. Findings below, ordered by severity.

### Bug 1 — Lost user message + orphan temp entry when a new chat's stream fails before `started`

`src/App.tsx:800-812` (`runStream` `finally`) combined with `src-tauri/src/commands.rs:992-1035` (`send_message`).

For a brand-new chat, `send` allocates a negative temp id and `runStream` runs with `startId < 0`. The `started` event is what migrates temp→real, and it is only emitted at `commands.rs:1035` — *after* `create_conversation` (`:1001`) and `save_messages` (`:1018`), both of which can return `Err` via `?` and reject the `sendMessage` promise before any `started` fires.

When that happens, `runStream`'s `catch` sets the error, then `finally` runs with `session.convId` still equal to the negative temp id:
- `reloadConv(tempId)` → `api.loadConversation(negativeId)` → backend matches no rows → returns empty messages → `setConvMessages(tempId, [])` **wipes the user's just-typed message**.
- The temp-id entry is never migrated, so `convStore` keeps an orphan negative-id key and `activeConvId` stays negative (composer/export stay disabled because they gate on `> 0`).

Real triggers: DB write failure, or anything that errors in `send_message` before line 1035. Existing chats are unaffected (their id is real and positive, so `reloadConv` works). Suggest guarding the `reloadConv`/artifact block on `fid > 0`, or only reloading once a real id is known.

### Bug 2 (minor) — `cancel_stream` no-op window at turn start

`src-tauri/src/commands.rs:1177` then `:1185`. The cancel token is inserted into the HashMap only after `state.mcp.tool_specs().await` (line 1177) completes. A `cancel_stream(conversation_id)` arriving during that pre-insert window finds no token and silently does nothing, so the stream isn't cancelled. Narrow window, low impact, but it is a real gap that did not exist conceptually before (the old code also set the token late, so this is pre-existing rather than introduced — flagging for completeness).

### Concurrency review — clean areas

- **Backend HashMap token lifecycle (focus #4):** `run_turn` returns `(Option<i64>, bool)`, not a `Result`, and has no `?`/early `return` inside the body — every loop exit is a `break`, so the `state.cancel.lock().unwrap().remove(&conversation_id)` at `commands.rs:1364` always executes. No token is left behind on error or normal completion. `cancel_stream` removes-and-cancels only the keyed entry. Correct.
- **Concurrent `run_turn`s sharing AppState (focus #4):** all `storage`/`config`/`auto_approved`/`pending_approvals`/`cancel` locks are short synchronous critical sections with the guard dropped before every `.await` (verified in `run_turn`, `request_approval`, `load_conversation`, the usage-record block). `pending_approvals` is keyed by globally-unique tool_call id; `auto_approved` and `cancel` are keyed by `conversation_id`. No cross-conversation corruption or deadlock.
- **`convStore` isolation (focus #3):** every mutator (`patchConvMessages`, `setConvMessages`, `setConvStreaming`, `clearPending`) is keyed by id and uses functional `setState`, so two concurrent streams writing different ids cannot clobber each other. Each stream carries its own `StreamSession` (`reasoning`/`content` buffers are no longer shared refs — the old global `reasoningBuf`/`contentBuf` are fully removed, verified by grep).
- **`activeRequests` balance (focus #3):** `+1` at `runStream` start (`:791`), `-1` clamped at `Math.max(0, …)` in `finally` (`:808-811`). `finally` always runs, so increments and decrements are balanced on both success and error paths.
- **temp→real migration (focus #1):** the `started` handler (`:608-633`) is a no-op when `tempId === realId` (existing chats), migrates `convStore` and `pendingByConv` atomically in functional updaters, advances `session.convId` synchronously, and follows `activeConvId` only via `cur === tempId ? realId : cur` so switching away mid-migration is respected. `nextTempId.current--` postfix-decrements per new chat, giving unique negative ids. Events are processed in channel order so `started` always precedes token/tool events. Correct (aside from the failure case in Bug 1).
- **Stale closures (focus #2):** `runStream`'s `finally` correctly reads `session.convId` (the migrated real id) rather than a captured `activeConvId`, and gates the artifact auto-open on `fid === activeConvIdRef.current` (the live ref, not a stale capture) — so a background stream won't yank the dock open. `priorArtifactCodes` is snapshotted from `convStore[startId]` before streaming; for a new chat that's the pre-user-message state, which has no artifacts, so the snapshot is correct.
- **`selectConv` to a background-streaming conv (focus #5):** skips overwriting live messages (`if (!live)`) and rebuilds the sibling map from the committed DB snapshot. In-progress messages have `null` ids and are filtered out of `buildSiblingMap`, so the map is consistent. Correct.
- **Cleanup paths:** delete-conversation removes the `convStore` entry and clears pending; `stop()`/`exportConversation`/`canExport`/draft-save all correctly guard against negative temp ids.

### Known design tradeoff (not a bug)

`metrics` is a single global object. Concurrent streams interleave `usage`/`request_metrics`/`decode`/`ttft` updates (`App.tsx:699-724`, `:789-795`) — the dashboard shows whichever stream emitted last, not per-conversation values. This is display-only and consistent with the global metrics poller design; no state corruption. Worth confirming it matches intent.

The only change I'd consider blocking is **Bug 1**.

content.text:
Typecheck clean. I have everything needed for the report.

## Review: per-conversation concurrent streams

I reviewed all six changed files for correctness and concurrency bugs. `tsc --noEmit` is clean. Findings below, ordered by severity.

### Bug 1 — Lost user message + orphan temp entry when a new chat's stream fails before `started`

`src/App.tsx:800-812` (`runStream` `finally`) combined with `src-tauri/src/commands.rs:992-1035` (`send_message`).

For a brand-new chat, `send` allocates a negative temp id and `runStream` runs with `startId < 0`. The `started` event is what migrates temp→real, and it is only emitted at `commands.rs:1035` — *after* `create_conversation` (`:1001`) and `save_messages` (`:1018`), both of which can return `Err` via `?` and reject the `sendMessage` promise before any `started` fires.

When that happens, `runStream`'s `catch` sets the error, then `finally` runs with `session.convId` still equal to the negative temp id:
- `reloadConv(tempId)` → `api.loadConversation(negativeId)` → backend matches no rows → returns empty messages → `setConvMessages(tempId, [])` **wipes the user's just-typed message**.
- The temp-id entry is never migrated, so `convStore` keeps an orphan negative-id key and `activeConvId` stays negative (composer/export stay disabled because they gate on `> 0`).

Real triggers: DB write failure, or anything that errors in `send_message` before line 1035. Existing chats are unaffected (their id is real and positive, so `reloadConv` works). Suggest guarding the `reloadConv`/artifact block on `fid > 0`, or only reloading once a real id is known.

### Bug 2 (minor) — `cancel_stream` no-op window at turn start

`src-tauri/src/commands.rs:1177` then `:1185`. The cancel token is inserted into the HashMap only after `state.mcp.tool_specs().await` (line 1177) completes. A `cancel_stream(conversation_id)` arriving during that pre-insert window finds no token and silently does nothing, so the stream isn't cancelled. Narrow window, low impact, but it is a real gap that did not exist conceptually before (the old code also set the token late, so this is pre-existing rather than introduced — flagging for completeness).

### Concurrency review — clean areas

- **Backend HashMap token lifecycle (focus #4):** `run_turn` returns `(Option<i64>, bool)`, not a `Result`, and has no `?`/early `return` inside the body — every loop exit is a `break`, so the `state.cancel.lock().unwrap().remove(&conversation_id)` at `commands.rs:1364` always executes. No token is left behind on error or normal completion. `cancel_stream` removes-and-cancels only the keyed entry. Correct.
- **Concurrent `run_turn`s sharing AppState (focus #4):** all `storage`/`config`/`auto_approved`/`pending_approvals`/`cancel` locks are short synchronous critical sections with the guard dropped before every `.await` (verified in `run_turn`, `request_approval`, `load_conversation`, the usage-record block). `pending_approvals` is keyed by globally-unique tool_call id; `auto_approved` and `cancel` are keyed by `conversation_id`. No cross-conversation corruption or deadlock.
- **`convStore` isolation (focus #3):** every mutator (`patchConvMessages`, `setConvMessages`, `setConvStreaming`, `clearPending`) is keyed by id and uses functional `setState`, so two concurrent streams writing different ids cannot clobber each other. Each stream carries its own `StreamSession` (`reasoning`/`content` buffers are no longer shared refs — the old global `reasoningBuf`/`contentBuf` are fully removed, verified by grep).
- **`activeRequests` balance (focus #3):** `+1` at `runStream` start (`:791`), `-1` clamped at `Math.max(0, …)` in `finally` (`:808-811`). `finally` always runs, so increments and decrements are balanced on both success and error paths.
- **temp→real migration (focus #1):** the `started` handler (`:608-633`) is a no-op when `tempId === realId` (existing chats), migrates `convStore` and `pendingByConv` atomically in functional updaters, advances `session.convId` synchronously, and follows `activeConvId` only via `cur === tempId ? realId : cur` so switching away mid-migration is respected. `nextTempId.current--` postfix-decrements per new chat, giving unique negative ids. Events are processed in channel order so `started` always precedes token/tool events. Correct (aside from the failure case in Bug 1).
- **Stale closures (focus #2):** `runStream`'s `finally` correctly reads `session.convId` (the migrated real id) rather than a captured `activeConvId`, and gates the artifact auto-open on `fid === activeConvIdRef.current` (the live ref, not a stale capture) — so a background stream won't yank the dock open. `priorArtifactCodes` is snapshotted from `convStore[startId]` before streaming; for a new chat that's the pre-user-message state, which has no artifacts, so the snapshot is correct.
- **`selectConv` to a background-streaming conv (focus #5):** skips overwriting live messages (`if (!live)`) and rebuilds the sibling map from the committed DB snapshot. In-progress messages have `null` ids and are filtered out of `buildSiblingMap`, so the map is consistent. Correct.
- **Cleanup paths:** delete-conversation removes the `convStore` entry and clears pending; `stop()`/`exportConversation`/`canExport`/draft-save all correctly guard against negative temp ids.

### Known design tradeoff (not a bug)

`metrics` is a single global object. Concurrent streams interleave `usage`/`request_metrics`/`decode`/`ttft` updates (`App.tsx:699-724`, `:789-795`) — the dashboard shows whichever stream emitted last, not per-conversation values. This is display-only and consistent with the global metrics poller design; no state corruption. Worth confirming it matches intent.

The only change I'd consider blocking is **Bug 1**.

The reviewer confirmed the concurrency design is sound, but found one real bug worth fixing (Bug 1): if a brand-new chat's request fails **before** the `started` event (e.g. a DB error in `send_message`), `runStream`'s `finally` runs with `session.convId` still the negative temp id — and `reloadConv(tempId)` loads an empty conversation, wiping the user's just-typed message. content.text:
Both clean. Here's what I did.

## Summary

Converted Fornax's chat streaming from **app-global single-flight** to **per-conversation concurrent streams**. You can now send a prompt in one chat and start another in a different thread while the first is still generating — each thread renders its own tokens live, and the sidebar shows a pulsing dot on any chat that's mid-stream.

### Backend (`src-tauri/`)
- **`state.rs`**: `cancel: Mutex<Option<CancellationToken>>` → `Mutex<HashMap<i64, CancellationToken>>`, keyed by `conversation_id`.
- **`commands.rs`**: `cancel_stream(conversation_id)` now cancels just that chat's token; `run_turn` registers its token by id at the very top (before any `.await`, so a cancel during MCP/tool discovery isn't a no-op) and removes it on exit. Tauri already dispatched these commands concurrently — the shared cancel slot was the only thing serializing them.

### Frontend (`src/`)
- **`App.tsx`**: replaced the single `messages` array + `streaming` boolean + shared reasoning/content buffers with a per-conversation store (`convStore: Record<id, {messages, streaming}>`). Each in-flight request carries its own `StreamSession`, so events route to the right chat regardless of which one is on screen. Brand-new chats use a negative temp id until the backend's `started` event delivers the real `conversation_id`, then the entry migrates temp→real. Tool approvals are keyed per-conversation; `messages`/`streaming`/`pendingApproval` are derived from the active chat.
- **`Sidebar.tsx`** + **`styles.css`**: a per-conversation streaming indicator dot.
- **`lib/tauri.ts`**: `cancelStream(conversationId)`.

### Verification
- `tsc --noEmit`, `vite build`, and `cargo clippy -D warnings` all clean.
- Ran a review subagent: it confirmed the concurrency design is sound (token lifecycle, store isolation, `activeRequests` balance, stale-closure handling, `selectConv` to a background stream) and caught one real bug — a failed brand-new-chat request (error before `started`) would reload an empty conversation and wipe the user's typed message. **Fixed** by skipping the post-turn reload when the id is still a negative temp id (keeps the optimistic messages alongside the error toast).

### Known tradeoff (not a bug)
The **metrics dashboard** is still a single global object — concurrent streams interleave their decode/TTFT/usage updates, so it shows the most recent emitter rather than per-conversation values. Making metrics per-conversation would be a separate change; tell me if you want it.

One thing I did **not** do: persist in-flight streams across an app restart, and I left the composer tied to the active chat only (you type in whichever chat is focused). Both seemed out of scope, but say the word if you want either.
